name: Daksha
description: Daksha is a prominent figure in Hindu mythology, often depicted as a Prajapati, a progenitor of humankind. He is considered one of the sons of Brahma, born from his right thumb. Daksha is renowned for his role in the creation of the universe and is seen as a powerful king and a skilled administrator. He is also known for his grand yajnas (sacrifices). A significant event associated with Daksha is the Daksha Yajna, where he insulted his daughter Sati and her husband, Shiva. This act led to Sati's self-immolation, triggering Shiva's wrath and the destruction of Daksha's Yajna. Daksha was later beheaded by Virabhadra, a fierce warrior created by Shiva, but was subsequently revived with a goat's head as a replacement. His lineage is vast, with numerous daughters who married various deities and sages, contributing to the expansion of the universe's population. He embodies the principles of dharma (righteousness) and plays a crucial role in the cosmic order. Despite his flaws, particularly his pride and animosity towards Shiva, Daksha remains a central figure in Hindu cosmology and genealogical narratives. He is often invoked during ceremonies and sacrifices, and his story serves as a cautionary tale about the consequences of arrogance and disrespect.
subtitle: Progenitor and Lord of Creation

keyMyths.title: Daksha Yajna
keyMyths.description: Daksha organized a grand sacrifice but intentionally excluded Shiva. His daughter Sati, Shiva's consort, felt deeply insulted and immolated herself in the sacrificial fire.

keyMyths.title: Birth of Daksha
keyMyths.description: Daksha was born from the right thumb of Brahma, the creator god. He was tasked with the responsibility of populating the universe.

keyMyths.title: Revival with Goat Head
keyMyths.description: After being beheaded by Virabhadra, Shiva's avatar, Daksha was revived by Shiva. He was given a goat's head as a sign of repentance and mercy.

sources.author: Vayu
sources.description: Details Daksha's lineage and role in creation.

worshipPractices.name: Yajnas (Sacrifices)
worshipPractices.description: Daksha's yajnas were grand, elaborate rituals performed to appease the gods and maintain cosmic order. These sacrifices often involved offerings of valuable items and were considered crucial for prosperity and well-being. However, his exclusion of Shiva from one such yajna led to significant consequences.

cultural.modernLegacy: Daksha's stories continue to be retold in various forms of media, including television series and films based on Hindu epics. His character serves as a reminder of the importance of humility and respect in religious practices. The Daksha Yajna narrative remains a potent symbol of the conflict between tradition and devotion.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Daksha's birth is described in various Puranas, most commonly as originating from the right thumb of Brahma, the creator god. This divine origin establishes him as a Prajapati, one of the progenitors of humankind and a key figure in the creation and ordering of the universe. Daksha's lineage connects him directly to the source of creation, granting him immense power and responsibility. He is often depicted as a wise and capable ruler, skilled in the arts of governance and diplomacy. His role as a Prajapati is central to understanding his actions and motivations within the complex tapestry of Hindu mythology. The circumstances of his birth also highlight the intricate and often symbolic nature of creation myths in Hinduism.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The most significant myth associated with Daksha is the Daksha Yajna. Daksha, in his arrogance, organized a grand sacrifice but intentionally excluded his daughter Sati's husband, Shiva. This act deeply offended Sati, who felt humiliated by her father's disrespect towards her husband. Unable to bear the insult, Sati immolated herself in the sacrificial fire. Upon learning of Sati's death, Shiva became enraged and sent Virabhadra to destroy Daksha's yajna and punish him. Daksha was eventually beheaded, but later, at the request of the other gods, Shiva restored Daksha's life, replacing his head with that of a goat. This story highlights the consequences of pride and disrespect, as well as the power and wrath of Shiva.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: While Daksha is not typically worshipped in the same way as major deities like Vishnu or Shiva, his role as a Prajapati and his association with important events in Hindu mythology give him a significant place in the Hindu pantheon. His story is often recited and reflected upon during religious discourses and festivals, particularly those related to Shiva and Sati. The Daksha Yajna narrative serves as a cautionary tale about the importance of humility and devotion. The story also highlights the interconnectedness of the gods and the consequences of disrupting the cosmic balance. Although no specific temples are dedicated solely to Daksha, he is often depicted in sculptures and paintings related to the Daksha Yajna and the stories of Shiva and Sati.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Daksha's legacy extends beyond his specific myths and stories. He represents the tension between tradition and devotion, and the dangers of pride and arrogance. His story continues to be relevant in contemporary discussions about religious practice and the importance of respecting different perspectives. The Daksha Yajna narrative has been adapted into various forms of art and media, including plays, films, and television series, ensuring that his story remains a part of the cultural consciousness. Daksha's character serves as a reminder that even those in positions of power and authority must act with humility and respect for the divine.

shortDescription: A Prajapati in Hindu mythology, Daksha is a creator god, son of Brahma, and father of Sati. He is known for his grand sacrifices and conflicts with Shiva.
